title: The Hesychast
narrative: An elder sits in silence. He does not look up when you enter. A single oil lamp burns. You have been standing here for what feels like hours.

title: The Sinai Brotherhood
narrative: The monks at Sinai have not stopped for three days. Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God, have mercy on me, a sinner. The prayer moves through them like breath, like heartbeat. One turns to you: even here, nepsis does not cease.

choices.text: Ask them to pray for your enemies
Learn the prayer's rhythm by watching

title: The Persistent Thought
narrative: It came at dawn and it is still here at compline. Not evil — only a small, reasonable thought about something you should have done differently. The fathers called these logismoi: thoughts that enter to make their home. A single root, if unwatched, becomes a forest.

choices.text: Name it aloud and release it
Walk until the thought cannot follow

title: The Philokalia
narrative: Beneath the rubble: a water-damaged book. The cover reads Philokalia — love of the beautiful. Most pages have fused. But one passage survives: 'The nous, cleansed of passion, becomes capable of theoria — the direct vision of God.' You sit with it in the dark.

choices.text: Copy the passage onto your palm
Leave it for the next warrior
Sit and read until the light fails

title: Apatheia
narrative: The old man has been called a fraud, a coward, and worthless — twice, in front of his disciples. He pours another cup of water. Not from coldness. The fathers called this apatheia: not the death of feeling, but its purification. He feels everything and is moved by none of it.

title: The Basin of Water
narrative: The man who betrayed him arrived at dusk. The abba said nothing. He brought out a basin. He knelt and washed the man's feet in silence. No explanation was offered or required. What breaks you here is not the gesture — it is that he meant it.

choices.text: Ask the abba how he forgave

title: The Third Week
narrative: Forty days in the desert. The hunger came, became familiar, then receded into the background like weather. What remains is something the fathers called sobriety — the body no longer screaming, the nous grown quiet and alert. The enemy chose this moment to appear.

title: The Refusal
narrative: Three monks came seeking a spiritual father. He turned each one away, saying: I am not worthy to teach, and I have nothing to give. Two left arguing. One bowed, said 'forgive me,' and turned to go. The elder called after only the last one: Stay.

choices.text: Ask him what he saw in the third monk
Wait in silence until he speaks

title: Night Vigil
narrative: Three in the morning. The chapel is cold enough to see your breath. Two candles burn against an icon of Christ. The reader's voice has gone hoarse but continues. The body has given out several times and been gathered up again. This is what the fathers meant by love — not feeling, but staying.

choices.text: Kneel in the cold and do not move

title: The Reasonable Voice
narrative: The thought arrived at midday, which the fathers named the most dangerous hour. 'You have worked enough. Even God rested on the seventh day.' It was not wrong, exactly. It spoke like wisdom, like mercy. The test, Abba Moses wrote, is not the thought's content — it is its fruit.

choices.text: Test the thought by its fruit, not its face
Refuse to engage it and return to work
